commands.review_summary:
The PR adds a guarded `reader.releaseLock()` call to the Ollama stream cleanup block.

PR surface: Source +3. Total +3 across 1 file.

Reproducibility: no. high-confidence live reproduction is available. Source inspection shows current main obtains the Ollama response reader and does not release that reader lock, but no live Ollama stream or resource probe was supplied.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] The remaining blocker is contributor-supplied real behavior proof and maintainer acceptance of the narrow cleanup scope, not an automated code repair.

target.title: fix(ollama): release stream reader lock to prevent resource leak
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`extensions/ollama/src/stream.ts:1231` obtains a `ReadableStreamDefaultReader` but the finally block only calls `release()` (fetch guard) — never `reader.releaseLock()`. The codebase pattern (e.g. `http-error-body.ts`, `provider-http-errors.ts`, `voice-call/response-body.ts`) calls both `reader.cancel()` AND `reader.releaseLock()` in finally blocks.

This is **D3**: resource leak — unreleased reader lock holds stream open until GC.

## Changes

- `extensions/ollama/src/stream.ts`: add `reader.releaseLock()` in finally block alongside existing `release()` call

**Scope**: 3 lines. Purely defensive — `releaseLock()` is the established codebase pattern.

## Evidence

### Existing tests continue to pass — no regression

```
pnpm exec vitest run extensions/ollama/src/stream.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
```

The fix is a defensive lock release matching the established pattern used in `http-error-body.ts`, `provider-http-errors.ts`, and `voice-call/response-body.ts` — all of which call both `cancel()` and `releaseLock()`. No behavioral change.
